output_summary: aligned schemas for datasets: amazon_small, goodreads_small, metabooks_small; generated 2 schema correspondence groups.
profiled datasets: amazon_small, goodreads_small, metabooks_small.
Reused the blocking configuration from state.
Blocking uses `semantic_similarity` for goodreads_small_amazon_small, metabooks_small_amazon_small, metabooks_small_goodreads_small, blocking on title, author, publish_year.
Performance-wise, pair completeness ranges from 0.99 to 1.00 and candidate counts range from 149824 to 199823.
The config also records dataset id columns.

matcher mode `ml` with config source reused from state; evaluated pairs: id_columns, matching_strategies; selected scores: matching_strategies (threshold=0.78, f1=0.7636363636363636, threshold=0.72, f1=0.9003690036900369, threshold=0.76); produced 2 pair strategy entries.
This run was an initial draft of the pipeline adaptation.
The pipeline loads three parquet datasets for amazon_small, goodreads_small, and metabooks_small and applies LLM-based schema matching to rename Goodreads and MetaBooks columns to the Amazon schema.
The entity matching stage was configured for all three dataset pairs using EmbeddingBlocker candidate generation, pair-specific comparator sets, feature extraction, and MLBasedMatcher inference.

The matcher training logic performs per-pair feature generation from provided correspondence CSVs and selects the best classifier for each pair through GridSearchCV over RandomForest, LogisticRegression, GradientBoosting, and SVM models using F1 scoring.
The fusion setup added attribute fusers for title, author, publish_year, and publisher, all using longest_string.
The pipeline clusters pairwise correspondences with MaximumBipartiteMatching and writes correspondence CSV outputs to the output/correspondences directory.
The matching behavior shifted to a supervised, pair-specific entity resolution approach that combines embedding-based blocking with learned classifiers instead of relying on a single generic matcher.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an initial draft of the evaluation script.
The run aligned fused records to gold IDs by expanding _fusion_sources into eval_id values and then evaluated fields with semantic match functions chosen per attribute type.
Title, author, publisher, and language were grouped under tokenized_match for token-based text comparison.
Publish_year was evaluated with year_only_match for year-specific comparison.
Page_count was evaluated with numeric_tolerance_match for tolerant numeric comparison.
Genres and any detected list-like non-metadata columns were grouped under set_equality_match after list normalization.
The run added helper functions to parse _fusion_sources values from JSON-like strings or Python literals and to detect the dominant gold ID prefix.
The run added build_eval_view to expand fused rows into evaluation-aligned rows, prefer source IDs matching the gold prefix or explicit gold IDs, and fall back to cluster _id when needed.
The run sorted expanded evaluation rows by numeric _fusion_confidence and dropped duplicate eval_id values to keep the highest-confidence mapping.
The run added list-like column detection and normalization across fused and gold data while excluding ID and metadata fields.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 57.14%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
The run is mainly failing in fusion rather than blocking, with missing fused entities, incomplete attribute coverage, and poor value selection driving low evaluation accuracy.
The fused output is missing records for some gold ids and some existing fused rows contain contaminated values from different books.
Preprocessing and normalization are too weak, causing many near-match values to be scored as mismatches.
Overall accuracy is 0.5714285714285714 across 20 evaluated records and 140 attribute evaluations.
Genres is the worst attribute with genres_accuracy = 0.05, which strongly indicates a severe fusion omission or wrong non-union behavior for genres.
Author accuracy is 0.4 and title accuracy is 0.55, while publish_year and language are both 0.85, showing the biggest weakness is not uniform across attributes.
Several mismatches have fused_id = NaN and fused_value = null across publisher, publish_year, title, author, page_count, language, and genres for amazon_135479, goodreads_8611, and amazon_12422.
There are explicit wrong-value cases where a fused row exists but values come from a different book, including metabooks_206748 and metabooks_38336.
This run was an evaluation-driven refinement of the existing pipeline.
The pipeline replaced generic longest_string fusion for title, author, publisher, and publish_year with attribute-specific fusers named fuse_title, fuse_author, fuse_publisher, and fuse_publish_year.
The pipeline added explicit fusion coverage for the previously uncovered evaluated attributes page_count, language, and genres using fuse_page_count, fuse_language, and genres_union.

The pipeline introduced dataset preprocessing helpers and applied them before matching, including HTML decoding and normalization for title, author, publisher, language, genres, page_count, and publish_year.
The matching comparators were updated to use normalized fields title_norm, author_norm, and publisher_norm instead of the raw text fields for string comparison.

Fusion shifted away from generic representative selection toward normalized, attribute-aware consensus rules with shortest canonical selection for title, author, and publisher, majority-style numeric fusion for publish_year and page_count, majority normalization for language, and union-based aggregation for genres.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or repair.
The dominant strategy was to refine evaluation alignment and use attribute-specific comparison functions against an evaluation view expanded from _fusion_sources.

The run kept tokenized matching for title, author, publisher, and language, year-only matching for publish_year, numeric tolerance for page_count, and set equality for genres and other detected list-like fields.
Title, author, publisher, and language were grouped under tokenized_match behavior.
Publish_year was handled as a dedicated year-only field with year_only_match.
Page_count was treated as a numeric field with numeric_tolerance_match.
Genres and other detected list-like columns were grouped under set_equality_match after list normalization.
ID-bearing and internal columns such as id, _id, eval_id, _eval_cluster_id, and _fusion_confidence were excluded from list-like normalization.
The run preserved and reused build_eval_view to map fused rows to gold eval_id values through parsed _fusion_sources and cluster identifiers.
The source-id parsing helper continued to support JSON and ast parsing fallbacks for serialized source lists.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 56.43%; previous logged accuracy was 57.14%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
The dominant problem in this run is a fusion-level false negative issue where some gold entities are missing from the fused evaluation view entirely.
A secondary problem is weak attribute fusion selection, especially for publisher, title, page count, and genres.
Repeated debug events show missing_fused_value with fused_id equal to NaN for gold entities such as amazon_135479, goodreads_8611, and amazon_12422.

The same missing entities fail across multiple attributes including title, page_count, language, author, publisher, and genres, which indicates whole-record alignment failures rather than isolated attribute errors.
Blocking completeness is reported as approximately 0.994 to 0.998, so the evidence does not point to restrictive blocking as the main cause.
Matching F1 is reported as approximately 0.76 to 0.90, so matcher quality appears decent relative to the observed missing fused records.
Overall accuracy is 0.5642857142857143 across 140 evaluations with 79 correct results.
Publisher accuracy is 0.25, title accuracy is 0.55, page_count accuracy is 0.60, and genres accuracy is 0.05.
This run was a refinement in evaluation_refinement mode focused on fixing fusion behavior exposed by the last evaluation.
Title fusion was redesigned to stop preferring the shortest variant and instead rank grouped normalized titles by supporter count, lower noise, and medium-length canonical forms.
Publisher fusion was rebuilt around stronger normalization, alias mapping, and canonical surface-form emission rather than shortest-string selection.
Page-count fusion was changed from exact majority voting to tolerance-based clustering with median selection within clusters.
Genre processing was expanded with canonical genre mapping, hierarchical enrichment, deduplication, and frequency-based retention in the final union.
Normalization rules were strengthened for titles, authors, and publishers, including removal of edition-style suffixes and additional publisher cleanup.
Publish-year fusion was adjusted to break ties with the median instead of relying only on exact majority outcomes.
The fusion strategy shifted away from shortest-string heuristics toward canonicalized agreement-based selection for title and publisher values.
Numeric fusion shifted from exact-match voting to near-agreement consensus for page counts.

ml_fused_standard_blocker = engine.run(; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an execution repair and refinement of the existing pipeline.
The pipeline further reworked title fusion by removing the shortest-string preference and using normalized agreement plus noise-aware surface selection in `choose_best_string` and `fuse_title`.

The pipeline expanded publisher normalization and canonical surface emission by adding more alias mappings such as `perennial classics`, `ballantine books`, `delacorte press`, and `harcourt brace`, and by ranking canonical publisher forms more strongly in `fuse_publisher`.

The pipeline broadened genre canonicalization and enrichment by adding mappings for `fiction`, `literature`, `children's`, `biography`, `memoir`, `classics`, `historical romance`, and `american`, and by expanding implied labels such as `Literature`, `Nonfiction`, and `Fiction` in `normalize_genres_value`.

The pipeline strengthened author handling by adding `split_authors` and token-level author counting support inside `fuse_author` while keeping grouped normalized author voting as the primary selection path.
The pipeline kept robust numeric fusion behavior, including positive-value validation for page counts and median-based year fallback, while preserving tolerance-style page count fusion logic.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 2; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or a repair.
The evaluation script was refined to improve evaluation-view ID alignment and reporting while keeping the same core attribute match functions.
The dominant strategy was to refine evaluation alignment by expanding fused rows against source provenance across all detected gold-ID prefixes instead of relying on a single dominant prefix.

The run preserved field-specific evaluation functions, using tokenized matching for title, author, publisher, and language, year-only matching for publish_year, numeric tolerance matching for page_count, and set equality for genres and other detected list-like fields.
Textual scalar fields were grouped under tokenized evaluation, specifically title, author, publisher, and language.
Temporal and numeric fields were handled with specialized evaluators, namely year_only_match for publish_year and numeric_tolerance_match for page_count.
Collection-like fields were grouped under exact set comparison, with genres explicitly evaluated by set_equality_match and additional detected list-like columns defaulting to the same function.
